title: Workplace Injuries
description: Workplace injuries are covered by workers' compensation in California and Arizona, but workers may also have claims against third parties. Understanding the difference between workers' comp and civil liability can significantly affect the compensation available to you.
metaTitle: Workplace Injury Guide — Workers' Comp & Your Legal Rights
metaDescription: What to do after a workplace injury in California or Arizona. Workers' comp, third-party claims, reporting deadlines, and when to speak with an attorney.

commonCauses.title: Slip, Trip, and Fall Accidents
commonCauses.description: Wet floors, uneven surfaces, poor lighting, and obstacles in work areas are a leading cause of workplace injuries across all industries.

commonCauses.title: Overexertion and Repetitive Strain
commonCauses.description: Lifting, pushing, pulling, and repetitive motion injuries account for a significant percentage of workers' compensation claims.

commonCauses.title: Struck-By Accidents
commonCauses.description: Workers struck by falling objects, moving equipment, or vehicles in warehouses and industrial settings.

commonCauses.title: Machinery and Equipment Accidents
commonCauses.description: Contact with unguarded machinery, power tools, or conveyor systems can cause severe lacerations, crush injuries, and amputations.

commonCauses.title: Vehicle Accidents on the Job
commonCauses.description: Workers injured while driving company vehicles or operating forklifts and other industrial vehicles.

commonCauses.title: Workplace Violence
commonCauses.description: Assaults by coworkers, customers, or third parties on the job can qualify for workers' compensation coverage.

commonCauses.title: Chemical and Toxic Exposure
commonCauses.description: Exposure to hazardous chemicals, fumes, and substances without adequate PPE can cause acute injuries and long-term illness.

immediateSteps.title: Get emergency medical care if needed
immediateSteps.description: Call 911 for serious injuries. For less severe injuries, see a workers' comp-approved physician or go to urgent care.

immediateSteps.title: Report the injury to your supervisor immediately
immediateSteps.description: Notify your employer or direct supervisor the same day the injury occurs. Delayed reporting can jeopardize your workers' comp claim.

immediateSteps.title: Document the scene and circumstances
immediateSteps.description: Photograph the exact location, any equipment or hazards involved, and anything that contributed to the injury. Do this before the scene is cleaned or changed.

immediateSteps.title: File a workers' compensation claim form
immediateSteps.description: In California, complete a DWC-1 form. In Arizona, submit a workers' comp claim to your employer's insurer. Request a copy of the filed claim.

immediateSteps.title: Identify witnesses
immediateSteps.description: Names and contact information of coworkers or others who witnessed the injury.

immediateSteps.title: See an authorized workers' comp physician
immediateSteps.description: Workers' comp requires treatment by authorized providers in most cases. Using a non-authorized provider without permission may affect your benefits.

immediateSteps.title: Keep records of all treatment and communications
immediateSteps.description: Document all medical visits, prescriptions, and any communications with your employer or the workers' comp insurer.

immediateSteps.title: Consider whether any third party is responsible
immediateSteps.description: If a contractor, customer, equipment manufacturer, or anyone other than your employer contributed to the injury, a civil claim may be available alongside workers' comp.

items: Photos of the accident location and any hazards
Photos of any equipment, tools, or materials involved
Names and contact info of witnesses
Copy of DWC-1 form (California) or equivalent claim form (Arizona)
All medical records and treatment notes from authorized providers
All physician and specialist visit notes
Documentation of missed work and lost wages
Any prior safety complaints or reports filed
Information on any contractor, vendor, or third party present at the time of injury
Equipment manufacturer information if a defective product was involved

timelineRisks.action: Report your injury to your employer the same day. Unreported injuries can create disputes about causation and timeliness.
California requires filing a workers' comp claim within 30 days of injury to preserve all benefits. File DWC-1 immediately.
Arizona requires filing a workers' comp claim within 1 year of injury. Do not wait.
If a third party contributed to your injury, you generally have 2 years to file a civil claim. Workers' comp does not prevent these claims.
Workers' comp permanent disability ratings affect long-term benefits. Ensure you have an independent medical evaluation before accepting a final rating.

insuranceIssues.explanation: Workers' compensation covers medical treatment and partial lost wages regardless of who caused the accident. However, benefits are limited and do not include pain and suffering.
California and Arizona law prohibit retaliation against workers who file workers' comp claims. Termination or adverse action after a claim is a separate legal violation.
Workers classified as independent contractors may not be covered by workers' comp. Misclassification is common — the actual working relationship matters, not just the label.
If a non-employer (vendor, contractor, equipment maker) contributed to your injury, you may have a civil claim that allows recovery of pain and suffering beyond workers' comp.
Workers' comp cases can be settled via a Compromise and Release (C&R) or Stipulated Award. C&R closes all future workers' comp rights for the injury. Understand the implications before signing.

whenToGetLawyer: Your injury requires surgery, hospitalization, or long-term medical care
Your workers' comp claim was denied or disputed
Your employer is disputing that the injury occurred at work
A third party (contractor, equipment manufacturer) may have contributed
You have been offered a workers' comp settlement
Your employer has taken adverse action after your claim
You are unsure whether your employment classification affects your rights
